2 min read
2 hours ago

--

HTTP has a new method now. It’s called QUERY. It became official in June 2026 as RFC 10008.
The Problem With GET
GET sends data through the URL.

• URLs have a size limit — big filters don’t fit well.

• GET is cacheable, which is its biggest strength.

• Sending a body with GET is unreliable — some proxies drop it, some reject it. Even React Query doesn’t support a body with GET.
The Problem With POST
POST can carry a body, so people started using it for search/query APIs too.

• No size limit, no URL encoding issues.

• But POST means “create” by default — it’s not safe, not idempotent.

• Not cacheable by default either, even when you’re just reading data.
Summary:

• GET → cacheable, but limited and unreliable body support

• POST → good body support, but wrong meaning and no caching
Enter QUERY
QUERY takes the good parts of both:

• Safe — like GET, doesn’t change any data

• Idempotent — safe to retry

• Has a body — like POST, so you can send complex filters

• Cacheable — but the body becomes part of the cache key
Example
http
QUERY /api/v1/products HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json{
"category": "electronics",
"min_price": 500,
"max_price": 1500,
"tags": ["sale", "wireless"],
"sort": "price_descending"
}
• QUERY /api/v1/products → method is QUERY, resource is /api/v1/products

• Content-Type: application/json → body format is JSON

• Body → the actual filter: category electronics, price between 500–1500, tagged sale/wireless, sorted by price descending
Response:
json
[
{ "id": 101, "name": "Wireless Headphones", "price": 799 },
{ "id": 102, "name": "Bluetooth Speaker", "price": 1200 }
]Same idea as a search query — just sent properly, without cramming filters into a URL or misusing POST.
Sources

• RFC 10008 — The HTTP QUERY Method

• Kreya: The new HTTP QUERY method explained
